5e75221d-85ed-43d6-b2af-de5933c0425e.text:
SentiBubbles: Topic Modeling and Sentiment Visualization of Entity-centric Tweets

João Oliveira, Mike Pinto DEI-FEUP University of Porto R Dr. Roberto Frias Porto, Portugal pssc@fe.up.pt
Pedro Saleiro, Jorge Teixeira DEI-FEUP, LIACC University of Porto R Dr.

56944a7d-f4da-4416-85ec-255ed4d25dc0.label: text_chunk
56944a7d-f4da-4416-85ec-255ed4d25dc0.text:
Roberto Frias Porto, Portugal pssc@fe.up.pt

ABSTRACT

Social Media users tend to mention entities when reacting to news events. The main purpose of this work is to create entity-centric aggregations of tweets on a daily basis. By applying topic modeling and sentiment analysis, we create data visualization insights about current events and people reactions to those events from an entity-centric perspective.

1.

60a0ca60-ce41-4a68-9d7b-104819fb7dde.label: text_chunk
60a0ca60-ce41-4a68-9d7b-104819fb7dde.text:
INTRODUCTION

Entities play a central role in the interplay between social media and online news [1]. Everyday millions of tweets are generated about local and global news, including people re- actions and opinions regarding the events displayed on those news stories. Trending personalities, organizations, compa- nies or geographic locations are building blocks of news sto- ries and their comments.

35e05991-a6ae-4735-ae16-e8bd553833a5.label: text_chunk
35e05991-a6ae-4735-ae16-e8bd553833a5.text: We propose to extract entities from tweets and their associated context in order to understand what is being said on Twitter about those entities and con- sequently to create a picture of people reactions to recent events.With this in mind and using text mining techniques, this work explores and evaluates ways to characterize given en- tities by finding: (a) the main terms that define that entity and (b) the sentiment associated with it.

b9c01e39-0728-41d1-88fa-28bfb11c2a02.label: text_chunk
b9c01e39-0728-41d1-88fa-28bfb11c2a02.text: To accomplish these goals we use topic modeling [2] to extract topics and relevant terms and phrases of daily entity-tweets aggrega- tions, as well as, sentiment analysis [3] to extract polarity of frequent subjective terms associated with the entities. Since public opinion is, in most cases, not constant through time, this analysis is performed on a daily basis.

b4910426-c2c2-4ff0-9d20-1df314d61f3c.label: text_chunk
b4910426-c2c2-4ff0-9d20-1df314d61f3c.text:
Finally we cre- ate a data visualization of topics and sentiment that aims to display these two dimensions in an unified and intelligible way.The combination of Topic Modeling and Sentiment Anal- ysis has been attempted before: one example is a model called TSM - Topic-Sentiment Mixture Model [4] that can
be applied to any Weblog to determine a correlation between topic and sentiment.

bd8a5bbf-b8b6-4e4e-91da-1069be0358cd.label: text_chunk
bd8a5bbf-b8b6-4e4e-91da-1069be0358cd.text:
Another similar model has been pro- posed proposed [5] in which the topic extraction is achieved using LDA, similarly to the model that will be presented. Our work distinguishes from previous work by relying on daily entity-centric aggregations of tweets to create a meta- document which will be used as input for topic modeling and sentiment analysis.

2.

dcbbb25e-ab25-47c4-a1ef-6816e5ba8e7d.label: text_chunk
dcbbb25e-ab25-47c4-a1ef-6816e5ba8e7d.text:
METHODOLOGY

The main goal of the proposed system is to obtain a char- acterization of a certain entity regarding both mentioned topics and sentiment throughout time, i.e. obtain a classifi- cation for each entity/day combination.

2.1 Tweets Collection

Figure 1 depicts an overview of the data mining process pipeline applied in this work.

0da7baeb-ca0d-4611-b124-822f1920980a.label: text_chunk
0da7baeb-ca0d-4611-b124-822f1920980a.text: To collect and process raw Twitter data, we use an online reputation monitoring plat- form [6] which can be used by researchers interested in track- ing entities on the web. It collects tweets from a pre-defined sample of users and applies named entity disambiguation [7]. In this particular scenario, we use tweets from January 2014 to December 2015.

3f2691b5-a91c-4ada-a901-d4f12eed0d8a.label: text_chunk
3f2691b5-a91c-4ada-a901-d4f12eed0d8a.text: In order to extract tweets related to an entity, two main characteristics must be defined: its canonical name, that should clearly identify it (e.g. “Cris- tiano Ronaldo”) and a set of keywords that most likely re- fer to that particular entity when mentioned in a sentence (e.g.“Ronaldo”, “CR7”). Entity related data is provided from a knowledge base of Portuguese entities.

e5d257c0-b083-41f6-a0c6-6a156b717374.label: text_chunk
e5d257c0-b083-41f6-a0c6-6a156b717374.text:
These can then be used to retrieve tweets from that entity, by selecting the ones that contain one or more of these keywords.

2.2 Tweets Pre-processing

Before actually analyzing the text in the tweets, we apply the following operations:

1. If any tweet has less than 40 characters it is discarded.

 These tweets are considered too small to have any meaningful content;

2.

fd361b4d-45bf-443e-8cc3-20e35bff0a96.label: text_chunk

f7580773-02be-4585-b13b-a34c84bbef60.label: text_chunk

acceeb1a-29f1-4ac3-95a3-4139b84b54bd.label: text_chunk

de544519-3624-48b3-a031-d8b9c91238f6.label: text_chunk
de544519-3624-48b3-a031-d8b9c91238f6.text:
2.3 Topic Modeling

Topic extraction is achieved using LDA, [2] which can de- termine the topics in a set of documents (a corpus) and a document-topic distribution. Since we create each document in the corpus containing every word used in tweets related to an entity, during one day, we can retrieve the most relevant topics about an entity on a daily basis. From each of those topics we select the most related words in order to identify it.

b3c395aa-f9f8-4096-8b48-b99202eaa42b.label: text_chunk
b3c395aa-f9f8-4096-8b48-b99202eaa42b.text:
The system supports three different approaches with LDA,


yielding varying results: (a) creating a single model for all entities (i.e. a single corpus), (b) creating a model for each group of entities that fit in a similar category (e.g. sports, politics) and (c) creating a single model for each entity.

3481386a-1375-40c7-86ed-e715ac9381d9.label: text_chunk
3481386a-1375-40c7-86ed-e715ac9381d9.text:
2.4 Sentiment Analysis

A word-level sentiment analysis was made, using Sentilex- PT [8] - a sentiment lexicon for the portuguese language, which can be used to determine the sentiment polarity of each word, i.e. a value of -1 for negative words, 0 for neutral words and 1 for positive words.

48388886-710e-40f5-920e-41dded9f6a19.label: text_chunk
48388886-710e-40f5-920e-41dded9f6a19.text:
A visualization system was created that displays the most mentioned words for each en- tity/day and their respective polarity using correspondingly colored and sized circles, which are called SentiBubbles.

3. VISUALIZATION

The user interface allows the user to input an entity and a time period he wants to learn about, displaying four sec- tions. In the first one, the most frequent terms used that day are shown inside circles (Figure 2).

9929b250-5422-415d-8c22-a17a4419734c.label: text_chunk
9929b250-5422-415d-8c22-a17a4419734c.text:
These circles have two properties: size and color. Size is defined by the term’s fre- quency and the color by it’s polarity, with green being pos- itive, red negative and blue neutral.

 Afterwards, it displays some example tweets with the words contained in the circles highlighted with their respective sentiment color. The user may click a circle to display tweets containing that word.

f3bdf841-45ad-4c80-8c6a-03ba03153d6c.label: text_chunk
f3bdf841-45ad-4c80-8c6a-03ba03153d6c.text:
A trendline is also created, displaying in a chart the number of tweets per day, throughout the two years analyzed. Fi- nally, the main topics identified are shown, displaying the identifying set of words for each topic.Figure 2: SentiBubbles visualization.

The relationships summarized from the knowledge graph highlight the concept of Knowledge Distillation and its significance in machine learning, particularly in the context of Neural Machine Translation (NMT).

1. **Knowledge Distillation and Student Model**: Knowledge Distillation is a process that results in the creation of a Student Model, which is specifically trained to mimic the performance of a Teacher Model. This relationship emphasizes the goal of transferring knowledge from a more complex model to a simpler one.

2. **Application to Neural Machine Translation (NMT)**: Knowledge Distillation is applied to enhance the efficiency and performance of Neural Machine Translation systems. It allows smaller models to replicate the behavior of larger, more complex models, thereby improving computational efficiency without sacrificing performance.

3. **Minimization of Cross-Entropy**: A key objective in Knowledge Distillation is to minimize Cross-Entropy. This metric quantifies the divergence between the probability distribution produced by the Teacher Model and the predictions made by the Student Model, highlighting the importance of aligning the two models' outputs.

4. **Generative Process and Data Distribution D**: Knowledge Distillation is also linked to the generative process involving the true target sequence derived from an underlying Data Distribution D. This relationship underscores the foundational role of data in training models and the importance of accurately representing the target outputs.

In summary, Knowledge Distillation serves as a crucial technique in machine learning, particularly for creating efficient models in NMT by minimizing discrepancies in predictions and leveraging data distributions effectively.
